results.possibleIdentity: Ellsworth Snyder (1931–2005), also known as E. C. Snyder, was an American pianist, visual artist, and experimental filmmaker active during the late 1960s and 1970s. He is best known for his long-term collaboration with composer John Cage and his association with the organization Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). Snyder directed the experimental short film "Colour Music: Opus 3 No. 1," which was screened at the 1968 Melbourne International Film Festival. He was a prominent interpreter of Cage's musical and theatrical works and co-authored "Two Texts Concerning John Cage: Chronology and Autobiography," a project published by E.A.T. in 1992. Based in Madison, Wisconsin, Snyder served as the music director for the First Unitarian Society and was a professor at Milton College. His artistic practice also included visual works made with paper, and his interviews and chronological research on John Cage remain significant resources in contemporary music studies.

results.possibleIdentity: David Turetsky (associated with David Turetsky Associates, Inc.) was a professional active in the late 1960s and early 1970s in New York City, working at the intersection of computer technology and the arts. He is primarily known for his association with "Art technology for the 1970's," a field that bloomed through collaborations between engineers and artists, most notably via the organization Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). Turetsky's firm, David Turetsky Associates, Inc., developed "BYTE-FORTRAN," a multipurpose software package for the IBM System/360. This software provided enhanced input/output (I/O) facilities, including advanced printer carriage control and sensing operations, which were essential technical components for early computer-generated art and plotter-based graphics of that era. His work in the late 1960s aligned with the technical breakthroughs and application areas of art technology frequently documented in contemporary computer and data processing publications like "Modern Data."

results.possibleIdentity: Jean Toche (born Jean Xavier Van Imschoot, 1932–2018) was a Belgian-born American artist, poet, and radical political activist prominent in the New York art scene during the late 1960s and 1970s. He is best known as a co-founder of the Guerrilla Art Action Group (GAAG), established in October 1969 alongside Jon Hendricks and Poppy Johnson. Toche’s work was characterized by "actions" (provocative performance art) aimed at institutional critique, such as the 1969 "Blood Bath" protest at the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) to protest the Vietnam War and the influence of the Rockefeller family. He was also a member of the Art Workers Coalition (AWC) and was famously arrested in 1970 for flag desecration following "The People’s Flag Show" at Judson Memorial Church. Historically, he is associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), appearing in their archives as a member and collaborating with E.A.T. engineer Per Biorn in 1970. In his later years, he focused on mail art and conceptual photo-text combinations addressing global political issues.

results.possibleIdentity: Gary Allen is a multi-disciplinary artist and writer who was active in the late 1960s and early 1970s, primarily associated with the artistic community at SUNY New Paltz during its "Golden Age." He was involved with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), an organization that promoted collaboration between artists and engineers. Allen's artistic practice included painting, photography, illustration, and postal (mail) art. He often integrated scientific and mathematical principles into his work, such as using mathematics to generate color sequences for his paintings and designing specialized lighting systems. Alongside his professor and collaborator Robert Schuler, Allen was a partner in a business that designed and constructed experimental architectural structures made of sprayed urethane foam. He contributed technical information on polyurethane foams and dome structures to "The Dome Builders Handbook" (1973). In his later career, Allen transitioned into a prolific writer, editor, and food historian, authoring or editing approximately 40 books, including "The Business of Food," "Human Cuisine," and "Herbs: A Global History."
